WALKERLAKE.EVENTS The CauseArtistsVisit

Walker Lake & Bighorn Sheep Resilience in the Desert

July 1–31, 2026

The Bighorn Crossing
847 Frontage Rd, Walker Lake, NV 89415

The Cause

Walker Lake has given up more than 150 feet of itself within a lifetime. The bighorn herd — Nevada’s largest — still crosses the road nearby.

This July, around twenty artists — painters, printmakers, ceramicists, metalworkers, some local, some from across the country — take over The Bighorn Crossing for a month of art about this lake.

Free, all month, indoors and across the outdoor grounds. In connection with Artown, in partnership with the Walker Basin Conservancy, working to return water to the lake.

Curated by Micky Kim · The Bighorn Crossing

Highlights

Virginia Dumas

Sculpture & poetry · Hawthorne, NV

Steel, bronze, clay, and stone — with verse written to stand beside them. Her Four Smiles, foundry-cast in silicon bronze, hangs in the show. She came to art through the foundry, casting and welding, and taught art, writing, and drama for years.

Patricia L. Cooke

Mixed-media fibers · Miami, FL

Hand-dyed, quilted color pushed toward beautiful excess. In cheeky, a herd of bighorn dance through rabbit brush while a scaled body — Cecil the Serpent? — slithers through the composition. Cooke is Senior Lecturer of Sculpture at the University of Miami.

Joe Taylor

Sculpture · Red Lodge, MT

Colorful, large-scale stoneware that draws on the body and the natural world — “ambiguous and playful spaces” of curiosity and wonder. A long-term resident artist at the Red Lodge Clay Center, and the son of Walker Lake artist Jack Taylor. Photo: Chris Autio.

Zachary Fields

Mokuhanga woodblock print · Pontotoc, MS

Non-traditional Mokuhanga — the Japanese tradition of water-based woodblock — carved by hand, each material showing its own character, recognized by the International Mokuhanga Conference. Trained also in geology, he reads the land in layers and time.

Gary A. Funk

Pen & ink · Hawthorne, NV

Dot by dot, in stippled ink, from photographs he takes himself at Walker Lake — a loving series of bighorn drawn to preserve the area’s culture and history. He has lived in Babbitt and Hawthorne nearly his whole life.
